title: Super-Earth Found Near Cosmic Neighbor
story: A giant planet was hiding right in our cosmic backyard! In 2018, astronomers made an exciting discovery on November 14. They found a Super-Earth planet orbiting Barnard's star, one of our closest stellar neighbors. This massive world is over three times bigger than Earth and circles a dim red dwarf star. At just six light-years away, it's practically next door in space terms. The find shows there's still plenty to explore in our galactic neighborhood!
activity_question: What type of star does the newly discovered Super-Earth orbit?
follow_up_question: How many times bigger is this Super-Earth compared to our planet?

title: Kilauea's Fiery Spectacle Lights Up Hawaii
story: Lava fountains shot into the sky as Kilauea volcano unleashed its most spectacular eruption in 1959. On November 14, Hawaii's Big Island trembled with excitement and a touch of fear. Bright orange rivers of molten rock flowed down the slopes, creating a dazzling display visible for miles. Scientists flocked to study this rare event, gathering valuable data about Earth's fiery inner workings.

story: Zoom! The rocket engines roared to life as Apollo 12 blasted off into space. title: Wandering World Found in Space
story: Astronomers made an exciting discovery in 2012 on November 14. They found a planet roaming through space all alone, without a star to orbit. This cosmic wanderer, named CFBDSIR 2149-0403, is the closest rogue planet ever spotted near Earth. It floats freely about 100 light-years away from us. Imagine a giant ball drifting through the darkness of space, with no sun to call home!
activity_question: What kind of planet did astronomers discover?
follow_up_question: Why do you think its called a rogue planet?

title: Hidden Giant: Greenland's Massive Meteorite Crater
story: Scientists made a jaw-dropping discovery in 2018. Buried deep under Greenland's Hiawatha Glacier, they found a massive crater. This wasn't just any hole in the ground. It was a colossal impact site, stretching about 31 kilometers wide. The culprit? An enormous iron meteorite that crashed into Earth long ago. This cosmic scar had been hiding beneath the ice, waiting to reveal its secrets on November 14.
activity_question: What was found under the Hiawatha Glacier in Greenland?
follow_up_question: How wide was the crater discovered under the glacier?

title: Ancient Ancestors Cooked Up a Tasty Discovery
story: Imagine biting into a perfectly cooked fish fillet. Now, picture our ancestors doing the same thing 780,000 years ago! In 2022, scientists made an exciting discovery at Gesher Benot Ya'aqov in northern Israel. They found the earliest evidence of humans using fire to cook their food. By studying ancient fish remains, researchers uncovered this tasty piece of human history on November 14.
activity_question: What did scientists discover evidence of in northern Israel?
activity_choices: The earliest use of fire for cooking

title: Whales Get a Safe Haven
story: Splash! A giant sperm whale dives deep into the Caribbean Sea. In 2023, these amazing creatures got some extra help. On November 14, the island of Dominica created a special protected area in the ocean. It's the world's first safe zone just for endangered sperm whales. Now these gentle giants have a place to swim, eat, and raise their babies without worrying about dangers from humans.
activity_question: What kind of whale is the protected area for?
bonus_fact: Sperm whales can dive deeper than any other whale, reaching depths of nearly two miles!

title: Nellie Bly's Race Around the World
story: Nellie Bly, a daring reporter, set out on an incredible adventure in 1889. She aimed to beat the fictional hero Phileas Fogg's 80-day trip around the world. On November 14, Bly boarded a ship in New York, kicking off her globe-trotting journey. She raced across oceans and continents, sending reports back home. Bly's determination paid off as she completed her whirlwind tour in just 72 days and 6 hours, proving that reality could outpace fiction!
activity_question: What was Nellie Bly's goal for her trip around the world?
bonus_fact: Nellie Bly's real name was Elizabeth Cochrane. She used a pen name for her reporting adventures.

title: The Scientist Who Changed Heart Health
story: A tiny baby born in Higashiyuri, Japan on November 14, 1933, would grow up to save millions of lives. Akira Endo became a brilliant biochemist who made a groundbreaking discovery. He developed the first statins, special medicines that help lower cholesterol and protect people's hearts. Endo's work revolutionized how doctors treat and prevent heart disease, making the world a healthier place.

title: Einstein's Bright Idea Shines
story: Imagine light as tiny packets of energy zipping through space! That's what Albert Einstein proposed in 1908 on November 14. His quantum theory of light changed how we understand the universe. Einstein suggested that light behaves both as a wave and as particles called photons. This groundbreaking idea helped explain tricky problems in physics and paved the way for modern technology like solar panels and lasers.

title: A Blazing Discovery in the Night Sky
story: Look, up in the sky! In 1680, astronomer Gottfried Kirch spotted something extraordinary - a dazzling comet streaking across the heavens. On November 14, he became the first person to see this celestial visitor, which would later be called the Great Comet of 1680. It was so bright that people could see it during the day! This cosmic spectacle amazed scientists and sky-watchers alike, leaving a lasting mark on astronomy.
activity_question: What did Gottfried Kirch discover in 1680?
bonus_fact: The comet was also called Kirch's Comet and Newton's Comet, honoring the astronomer and the famous scientist.

This unexpected performance stopped all voting on an important bill about the Treaty of Waitangi, New Zealand's founding document. On November 14, the usually formal chamber was filled with the energy and passion of Maori culture.

title: A Brave Stand at the Oscars
story: Sacheen Littlefeather made history in 1973 when she spoke at the Academy Awards. Born on November 14, 1946, this Native American activist declined an Oscar on behalf of Marlon Brando. She used the moment to highlight the treatment of indigenous people in films and real life. Her bold action sparked important conversations about representation in Hollywood.

activity_choices: Declined an Oscar on behalf of Marlon Brando
